preFix.title: Fitting a bath shower mixer tap
preFix.subtitle: Remove old bath taps and fit a new mixer tap with a shower handset attachment
preFix.excerpt: How to replace a pair of old bath pillar taps with a bath-shower mixer tap. Covers isolating the supply, removing the existing taps, fitting the new mixer body, and connecting the shower handset and hose.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text:
Step 1, Isolate the supply, drain the section, catch the residual.

Close the local isolating valves under the bath panel if there are any. If there are no isolating valves, close the main stopcock under the kitchen sink. Open the existing hot and cold taps fully to drain the supply pipes. Have a bowl and a thick towel under the bath ready; the tails of the taps hold 300 to 500 ml of water each.
Step 2, Disconnect and remove the old taps

Reach under the bath and disconnect the supply pipes from the tap tails, typically a compression nut or a flexible corrugated hose connected by a nut. Use the adjustable spanner, supporting the pipe to avoid pulling the soldered joint on the rigid pipe. Remove the
below each tap with the basin wrench. Lift the taps free. Clean the bath deck surface, old silicone sealant and scale need to come off before the new tap seals correctly.
Step 3, Fit the new mixer body

Most bath-shower mixers come with a rubber or silicone deck seal. Position the mixer body over the tap holes in the bath deck, threading the hot and cold tails through their respective holes. Apply the deck seal and the back plate, then thread on the backnuts hand-tight below the deck. Use the basin wrench to tighten firmly without over-torquing, cracking a ceramic or composite bath deck is the consequence of excessive force.

Connect the hot supply to the hot tail and the cold supply to the cold tail. Apply PTFE tape to any threaded male connections before fitting. Tighten to firm with the adjustable spanner, finger-tight plus one turn is too loose; two full turns past finger-tight is right for compression connections. If the existing pipes are too short or do not reach the new tap tails, a pair of flexible corrugated stainless tap connectors in 15 mm × 300 mm or 450 mm bridge most gaps.
Step 5, Fit the shower handset and bar

to the wall at the height specified by the tap manufacturer (typically 900 to 1100 mm from the top of the bath). Connect the shower hose from the mixer outlet to the handset. Restore the water supply and test: open the taps, check for leaks at every connection under the bath, and test the flow diverter (the button that switches from bath fill to shower mode).
Thermostatic mixer tap: the fitting process is the same. Check the preset temperature stop matches the household hot-water temperature before finishing the job.

Mono-block mixer (single-hole bath): the entire mixer fits in one 55 to 60 mm hole. The fitting procedure is the same but all connections are made through a single hole; a crow's-foot spanner is needed for the single central backnut.

items.fix: Turn off supply, remove the tap, re-seat the deck seal, and retighten the backnut.
items.cause: Rubber deck seal not seated flat, or backnut not tight enough
items.symptom: Leak from the deck seal under the tap body

items.fix: Check the supply pressure. Most bath-shower mixers are designed for high-pressure mains; some require a pressure-reducing valve at low-pressure installations.
items.cause: Inlet pressure too high for the diverter mechanism, or diverter valve worn
items.symptom: Shower diverter does not hold, bath tap continues to flow while shower is selected

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
